sections.heading: Overall use barely changed, but modern methods became more common
sections.body: Among all women aged 15 to 49, contraceptive use changed very little: 33.8 percent in 2025, compared with 34.1 percent in 2022. The bigger change was in the type of method used. Modern method use rose from 24.3 to 25.7 percent, while traditional method use fell from 9.8 to 8.1 percent. Nationally, the story is less about more women using contraception overall and more about current users shifting toward modern methods.

sections.heading: Regional gaps are much larger than the national change
sections.body: Davao Region had the highest share of all women using any contraceptive method, at 45.1 percent. BARMM had the lowest, at 16.2 percent. Central Visayas was also near the low end at 25.7 percent, and NCR was below the national level at 28.5 percent. This gap across regions is much wider than the national change between 2022 and 2025, making the regional pattern one of the clearest ways to understand the data.

sections.heading: Regions with higher use also tend to rely more on modern methods
sections.body: Regions with relatively high overall contraceptive use also tend to have high modern method use. Davao Region led in both any-method use at 45.1 percent and modern method use at 37.1 percent. On modern method use, it was followed closely by MIMAROPA at 33.9 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN at 32.5 percent, and Caraga at 32.2 percent. This shows that regional differences are not only about whether women use contraception, but also about whether use is mainly through modern methods.

sections.heading: Some regions fell while others increased since 2022
sections.body: Central Visayas had the largest drop in any-method use, falling from 37.1 percent in 2022 to 25.7 percent in 2025. Cagayan Valley also fell sharply, from 40.3 to 32.9 percent, while BARMM fell from 21.4 to 16.2 percent. Other regions moved the other way. Eastern Visayas, SOCCSKSARGEN, and Caraga each rose by 4.0 percentage points, while MIMAROPA rose by 3.9 points and Davao Region by 3.8 points.

sections.heading: Traditional methods matter more in some regions than nationally
sections.body: Nationally, traditional method use was 8.1 percent, but some regions were clearly above that level. Eastern Visayas was highest at 12.1 percent, followed by Ilocos Region at 11.8 percent and Western Visayas at 10.8 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had a different pattern: any-method use was below the national level at 30.2 percent, but most of that use was modern methods, with 24.7 percent modern and 5.6 percent traditional.

sections.heading: Use is highest among women in their 30s
sections.body: Among all women, contraceptive use rises sharply after the youngest age groups and peaks in the 30s. It was 3.1 percent among women 15 to 19 and 19.9 percent among women 20 to 24. It then reached 49.5 percent at ages 30 to 34 and 53.9 percent at ages 35 to 39. Use stayed high at 51.1 percent for ages 40 to 44, before dropping to 39.3 percent among women 45 to 49.

sections.heading: Use rises sharply as women have more living children
sections.body: The number of living children is one of the clearest differences outside the regional results. Any-method use was only 4.2 percent among women with no living children. It rose to 52.6 percent among women with one or two children and 66.8 percent among women with three or four children. The type of method also changes with family size: women with three or four children had 55.8 percent modern method use, compared with 2.4 percent among women with no living children.

sections.heading: Rural women used contraception more, but the gap was not large
sections.body: In 2025, rural women had higher contraceptive use than urban women: 36.3 percent compared with 31.9 percent. The gap was larger for modern methods, with rural women at 28.8 percent and urban women at 23.3 percent. Even so, the 4.4-point urban-rural gap in any-method use is much smaller than the gap across regions. This suggests that regional location matters more than urban or rural residence alone.

sections.heading: Education patterns are not a simple high-to-low ranking
sections.body: Contraceptive use was highest among women with Grades 1-6 education, at 47.9 percent, and was also high among women with post-secondary education, at 45.9 percent. It was much lower among women with Grades 11-12 education at 15.2 percent and among women with no education at 24.2 percent. Because the figures cover all women, these education differences should be read together with age and family-building stage, not as a simple ranking of advantage.

sections.heading: Use generally falls as household wealth rises, but region gaps are larger
sections.body: Any-method use generally declined as household wealth rose, from 39.6 percent in the lowest quintile to 28.4 percent in the highest. Modern method use showed the same pattern more clearly, at 32.5 percent in the lowest quintile and 20.0 percent in the highest. This difference matters, but it is still smaller than the gap between the highest- and lowest-use regions.

takeaways: National contraceptive use among all women was almost the same as in 2022, but users shifted more toward modern methods.
Regional differences stand out the most, with Davao Region far above BARMM and Central Visayas in any-method use.
Use is shaped by life stage as well as geography, rising sharply among women in their 30s and among women with living children.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
